title: Paper clay sculpted organic vessel
subtitle: An asymmetric curved vessel built from paper-clay coils and pinched walls
excerpt: An asymmetric vessel built up from paper-clay coils and pinched walls, allowed to find its own organic shape rather than a controlled silhouette. The cellulose fibres in paper clay bridge joins as they dry and let the walls take unusual curves without cracking. Air-dries at room temperature over three to four days. The finished vessel is decorative.
sourceNotes: Paper clay as a hand-building medium was developed for studio use in the late twentieth century; the cellulose-reinforcement principle appears in older traditions of straw-tempered earthenware. Technique derived from first principles. V&A and Wikimedia museum open-access collections referenced for general organic-vessel study.

glossaryTerms.definition: Clay body blended with cellulose fibres (paper pulp). The fibres reinforce the wet clay, bridge joins, and allow asymmetric or thin-walled forms that plain clay cannot hold.
The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to trim and join. It feels firm like thick leather.
Air-dried but unfired. The clay is pale and uniform in colour. For paper clay air-dry this is the finished structural state.
Clay diluted with water to a thick brushable consistency. Used as the bonding paste between joining surfaces.

content.text: An organic vessel takes its shape from the way the clay is built rather than from a planned silhouette. The wall curves where the coils bunch, narrows where the hand pinches, and finishes asymmetric.
Paper clay

is the right body for the job: the cellulose fibres reinforce the wet wall and bridge any joins as they dry. The same form in plain air-dry clay tends to crack at the asymmetric tension points. The vessel dries at room temperature over three to four days, no kiln required, and is decorative.
Preparation

Paper clay does not need long wedging; the fibres mean that air pockets do less damage. One or two minutes of light kneading is enough to bring the clay to an even consistency. Keep the bulk wrapped in plastic between sessions.
Building the vessel
1. Make the base

Take 150 g of clay and press it flat under the heel of the hand into an irregular oval around 10 cm across and 10 mm thick. Don't aim for a clean circle; the asymmetry of the base sets the asymmetry of the finished vessel. Set the base on the board and score the outer 5 mm of the top surface with the needle tool, ready for the first coil.
2. Add the first coils
Roll a coil 12 mm thick and 30 cm long between the palms and the board. Brush
slip

onto the scored base ring and press the coil into place around the edge. Cut the ends at a slight angle so they overlap and press together. Add two more coils on top, blending the inside down into the previous coil with the side of the finger. Build to a wall height of around 5 cm in this first session. Aim for a wall thickness of 8 mm; paper clay holds at this thickness better than plain air-dry.
3. Pinch and curve the walls

Once the third coil is on, work the wall with the fingers from the inside while supporting the outside with the other hand. Pinch the wall thinner in some places and leave it thicker in others; pull the rim outward in one spot and inward in another. Aim for a wall that varies between 6 mm and 10 mm thickness around the perimeter. The cellulose fibres in paper clay hold the wall together at the thin spots that plain clay would split.
4. Build to height, working asymmetric
Add coils up the wall in stages, letting the previous coil firm to
leather-hard

before adding the next if the wall starts to slump. Vary the placement: a coil set slightly outside the one below flares the wall; a coil set slightly inside narrows it. Don't worry about a clean silhouette. The finished vessel should be around 18 to 22 cm tall and asymmetric. Build the rim line uneven on purpose: higher on one side, dipping on another.
5. Finish the rim
Smooth the rim with a damp sponge to round and soften the cut edge. Leave any deliberate dips and rises in place. The uneven rim is part of the form, not a mistake to correct.
Working in stages

A vessel of this size needs two or three short sessions. Build to mid-height, cover loosely with plastic, leave for four to six hours so the wall firms enough to support the next layer. Score and slip the top coil before adding the next: paper clay bonds well to itself but only when both surfaces are scored and slipped. Dry coil pressed onto dry coil pops apart at the join.
Smoothing

Once the vessel is fully built and the wall has firmed to leather-hard, decide how smooth the surface should be. The choices are: smooth fully with the wooden rib for a continuous outside surface; leave the coil ridges visible for a coiled-pottery look; or smooth only in patches for a mixed surface. Press a damp sponge gently around the whole form to lift any clay dust and round any sharp edges.
Drying

Cover loosely with a single layer of plastic for the first twenty-four hours so the inside and outside dry at the same rate. Turn the vessel a quarter-turn every twelve hours so all sides dry evenly. Full drying to
bone-dry
takes three to four days at room humidity. Paper clay dries slightly lighter than plain air-dry. The surface picks up a fine fibrous texture as it hardens.
Finishing

Once bone-dry, sand any rough spots with 220-grit sandpaper outdoors or near an open window. Wear a mask: dry clay dust (and the dry fibres in paper clay) is irritating to breathe. Paint with acrylics, leave plain, or rub an earth-tone wash into the surface to bring out the texture. Seal with two thin coats of acrylic varnish brushed on cleanly. The seal protects the surface but does not make the vessel water-tight.
Care

Paper clay air-dry is not waterproof and the vessel will soften and crack if filled with water. For cut flowers, drop a glass test tube or florist water tube inside the vessel and arrange the stems into that. Keep the vessel indoors and away from damp surfaces. Wipe clean with a barely damp cloth.
Variations
Pull a small spout from the rim before the clay firms. Pinch a 2 cm section out and down to hint at a pouring lip. The vessel still is not water-tight, but the shape gains interest.
Add a small loop handle from a rolled coil scored and slipped to one side of the wall. The handle is decorative; do not lift the vessel by it.
Add tactile texture: press a thumb deeply into the wet wall in two or three places, leaving thumb-print depressions that read as deliberate marks.

items.symptom: Wall slumps before the form is finished
items.cause: Coils added too quickly; lower wall too soft to support the weight above
items.fix: Stop and let the lower wall firm under plastic for four to six hours; resume building once the lower wall feels firm but still cool to the touch

items.symptom: Wall cracks at an asymmetric tension point
items.cause: Wall pinched too thin in that spot, or the curve too sharp for the clay to hold
items.fix: Press fresh slip and a small patch of paper clay into the crack; smooth with the rib; dry under plastic for twelve hours

items.symptom: Coil join pops open as the wall dries
items.cause: Score-and-slip omitted, or surfaces too dry when joined
items.fix: Always score both surfaces and brush thick slip on both sides; press firmly and blend

items.symptom: Base cracks in a star pattern during drying
items.cause: Base dried much faster than the wall and shrank against the wall's hold
items.fix: Cover the base with plastic for the first twenty-four hours; if a crack opens, press fresh slip into it and dry slowly for twelve hours more
